title: SEGMENT 11 — The plasmid resistome and the environmental reservoir

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — The plasmid resistome and the environmental reservoir
I have used the word resistome several times.
Let me define it properly, because it is the central concept of the ecological view of antibiotic resistance.
The resistome is the collection of all antibiotic resistance genes — and proto-resistance genes that might evolve into clinically relevant resistance — in a given environment or community.
There is a clinical resistome: the resistance genes circulating in pathogens in our hospitals.
There is a commensal resistome: the resistance genes in the commensal bacteria of human and animal guts.
And there is an environmental resistome: the much larger and older reservoir of resistance genes in soil, water, sediment, and the bacterial communities of the natural world.
The environmental resistome was first systematically characterized by Gerry Wright's group at McMaster University in the late 2000s.
They cultured several hundred soil Streptomyces — the organisms that produce most of our antibiotics in the first place — and asked which antibiotics each strain was resistant to.
The answer was: essentially all of them.
Every Streptomyces they tested was resistant to multiple antibiotics, often including ones that the strain itself does not produce.
Soil bacteria are intrinsically multi-drug resistant, and they have been, on evolutionary timescales, for as long as antibiotic-producing organisms have existed in the soil.

The 2011 paper that drove the point home was by Vanessa D'Costa, Hendrik Poinar, and colleagues, who sequenced DNA from 30,000-year-old permafrost samples in the Canadian Arctic and found a diverse collection of antibiotic resistance genes — beta-lactamases, tetracycline resistance genes, glycopeptide resistance genes including a vanA homolog — preserved in permafrost from a time long before any human had ever used an antibiotic.
The resistance genes were there, in working order, in ancient bacterial DNA, 30,000 years before penicillin.
This is the cleanest possible demonstration of the central thesis: resistance is ancient.
So the picture is this.

The environmental resistome — soil bacteria, water bacteria, animal-gut bacteria all over the planet — contains an enormous reservoir of resistance genes that predate human antibiotic use by millions of years.
These genes evolved in microbial arms races that have been running since long before there was a phylum Chordata.
Our deployment of antibiotics globally has applied selection that mobilizes these ancient genes onto plasmids and transposons, captures them in integrons, and moves them into clinical pathogens.
The pipeline runs from soil, to commensals, to pathogens.
The plasmids are the conveyor belt.
This is why no resistance-control strategy that focuses only on human clinical prescribing can possibly succeed.
The reservoir is the entire bacterial biosphere.
You cannot decontaminate it.

The best you can do is reduce the selective pressure — use fewer antibiotics, use them more carefully, develop drugs to which resistance has not yet been mobilized — and slow the rate at which the environmental resistome leaks into your clinic.
